title: Ensure 'external scripts enabled' Database Flag for Cloud  SQL SQL Server Instance Is Set to 'off'

input_row.title: Ensure 'external scripts enabled' Database Flag for Cloud  SQL SQL Server Instance Is Set to 'off'
input_row.description: It is recommended to set external scripts enabled database flag for Cloud SQL SQL Server instance to off
input_row.rationale: external scripts enabled enable the execution of scripts with certain remote language extensions. This property is OFF by default. When Advanced Analytics Services is installed, setup can optionally set this property to true. As the External Scripts Enabled feature allows scripts external to SQL such as files located in an R library to be executed, which could adversely affect the security of the system, hence this should be disabled. This recommendation is applicable to SQL Server database instances.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the instance to open its Instance Overview . Ensure the database flag external scripts enabled that has been set is listed under the Database flags section. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Ensure the below command returns off for every Cloud SQL SQL Server database instance gcloud sql instances describe --format=json | jq '.settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="external scripts enabled")|.value' In the output, database flags are listed under the settings as the collection databaseFlags.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the SQL Server instance for which you want to enable to database flag. 3. Click Edit. 4. Scroll down to the Flags section. 5. To set a flag that has not been set on the instance before, click Add item, choose the flag external scripts enabled from the drop-down menu, and set its value to off. 6. Click Save to save your changes. 7. Confirm your changes under Flags on the Overview page. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Configure the external scripts enabled database flag for every Cloud SQL SQL Server database instance using the below command. gcloud sql instances patch --database-flags "external scripts enabled"=off Note: This command will overwrite all database flags previously set. To keep those and add new ones, include the values for all flags you want set on the instance; any flag not specifically included is set to its default value. For flags that do not take a value, specify the flag name followed by an equals sign ("=").
input_row.impact: Setting custom flags via command line on certain instances will cause all omitted flags to be reset to defaults. This may cause you to lose custom flags and could result in unforeseen complications or instance restarts. Because of this, it is recommended you apply these flags changes during a period of low usage.
input_row.default_value: By default external scripts enabled is off
input_row.additional_information: WARNING: This patch modifies database flag values, which may require your instance to be restarted. Check the list of supported flags - https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/sqlserver/flags - to see if your instance will be restarted when this patch is submitted. Note: some database flag settings can affect instance availability or stability, and remove the instance from the Cloud SQL SLA. For information about these flags, see Operational Guidelines." Note: Configuring the above flag restarts the Cloud SQL instance.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control can be fully verified using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command. The audit instructions provided specify that a single command can retrieve the necessary database flag configuration for Cloud SQL SQL Server instances, and there are no indicators that human judgment or SSH access is required. Thus, it meets the criteria for an automated approach.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_sql_instance_external_scripts_enabled_off

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command: `gcloud sql instances describe --format=json | jq '.settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="external scripts enabled")|.value'` to check the `external scripts enabled` flag state for all Cloud SQL SQL Server instances.
- The command will enumerate all Cloud SQL SQL Server instances and retrieve the configuration settings, specifically focusing on the `external scripts enabled` database flag.
- **Pass Criteria:** The command should return `off` for the `external scripts enabled` flag for all SQL Server instances. 
- **Fail Criteria:** If any instance returns a value other than `off`, it indicates a compliance failure.
- This will ensure that the security posture regarding the introduction of external scripts in Cloud SQL SQL Server instances is maintained as per the CIS recommendations.
step2_review:
**1. APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE  
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The use of the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command to check the database flag settings for Cloud SQL SQL Server instances is appropriate. This task does not require manual intervention or SSH access, as the necessary information can be retrieved programmatically using available GCP APIs.

**2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8  
- The automation details are mostly correct, but there is a minor issue with the command provided. The `gcloud sql instances describe` command requires the specification of an instance name to retrieve its configuration. The command should be adjusted to iterate over all instances.
- The program name follows a reasonable convention, but it could be more descriptive regarding the specific security check being performed.

**3. IMPROVEMENTS:**  
- **Command Correction:** The command should iterate over all instances. A more accurate approach would involve listing all instances first and then describing each one individually. For example:
  ```bash
  gcloud sql instances list --format="value(name)" | while read instance; do
    gcloud sql instances describe "$instance" --format=json | jq '.settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="external scripts enabled")|.value'
  done
  ```
- **Program Name Suggestion:** Consider renaming the program to `gcp_sql_sqlserver_external_scripts_disabled` to clearly indicate that the check is for SQL Server instances and that the desired state is 'disabled' or 'off'.
- **Missing Considerations:** Ensure that the script accounts for different regions or projects if applicable, as instances may be spread across them.

**4. CONFIDENCE:** HIGH  
- I am confident in the assessment, given the correct use of GCP SDK capabilities and the minor adjustments needed for technical accuracy.

**5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify  
- Modify the command to correctly iterate over all Cloud SQL SQL Server instances, ensuring that each instance is checked individually. Adjust the program name for clarity and specificity regarding the security check being performed.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** Both AI opinions agree that the task can be automated using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command. The necessary information to verify the 'external scripts enabled' flag is accessible programmatically through GCP APIs, and no manual intervention or SSH access is required. The second opinion correctly points out the need to iterate over all instances, which is a minor adjustment to the command.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
gcp_sql_sqlserver_external_scripts_disabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- GCP Resource/Service: Cloud SQL SQL Server Instances
- API/CLI Method: Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command
- Properties to Check: `.settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="external scripts enabled")|.value`
- Pass Criteria: The command should return `off` for the `external scripts enabled` flag for all SQL Server instances.
- Fail Criteria: If any instance returns a value other than `off`, it indicates non-compliance.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** There was no significant disagreement between the AI opinions; both agreed on the automated approach. The second opinion provided a more accurate command iteration method, which was adopted.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Google_Cloud_Platform_Foundation_Benchmark_v4_0_0
